Tafseer:
"Qad aflaha al-mu'minoon" (Successful indeed are the believers).

Glad tidings of worldly and otherworldly success to the believers:
Although the word "believers" is general, its primary referents here are those very believers who, in that era, were risking their lives and wealth for the sake of truth through migration and Jihad. It is stated that they have succeeded in attaining their goal. Although this glad tiding relates to the future, it is expressed in the past tense to indicate its absolute certainty, because once a matter is decided by Allah, it is as though it has already occurred. The true fruit of the success promised here is the success of the Hereafter. That is the ultimate goal, and it has always been the target of the believers. However, within this lies also the implicit promise of consolidation on earth, which was given in the previous Surah. Since that was mentioned in detail in the previous Surah, this Surah focuses on the ultimate reward—Firdaws (Paradise). Other blessings naturally follow beneath it.

"Alladhina hum fee salatihim khashi'oon" (Those who are humble in their prayers).

Pointer to the spirit of prayer:
"Khashu'" means humility, self-abasement, submissiveness, and modesty. This word is used in various forms in the Quran. Its occurrences in phrases like "the voices are humbled" and "that their hearts should become humble" shed light on the true spirit of the word; hence, the opinion of those who interpret it merely as physical stillness is, in our view, incorrect. This is a pointer to the true spirit of prayer upon which the aforementioned success depends. The meaning is that not only should a person's back and head bow before the Lord, but his heart must also submit. The state of these believers is such that in their prayers, their standing, their posture, their voice, their bowing, and their prostrations—every single thing—bears witness to the humility of their hearts. It should be kept in mind here that the final verse of the previous Surah directed the establishment of prayer, which is an expression of the outward care and adherence to prayer. In this Surah, the actual spirit of prayer is mentioned, and that too as an attribute of the believers who are being given this glad tiding of success.

"Walladhina hum 'ani al-laghwi mu'ridoon" (And those who turn away from ill speech/vanity).

The effect of prayer on life:
This describes the effect of prayer on life. "Laghw" (vanity/ill speech) refers to every word and deed that distracts a person from the true purpose of life—seeking the pleasure of Allah—regardless of whether it is otherwise permissible or impermissible. A prayer that contains humility inevitably influences a person's life such that he begins to avoid vain, unnecessary, useless, and aimless things. He is constantly anxious that if he commits any vain action, he has to face his Knower of the Unseen Master one day, and he is reminded of this night and day at least five times. It is obvious that a person whose conscience is so awake and sensitive that his nature feels aversion to every unnecessary movement will rarely commit any major indecency or evil. This effect of prayer on life is described in Surah Al-Ankabut thus: (Indeed, prayer prohibits immorality and wrongdoing) [Al-Ankabut: 45]. Prohibition means that it is a highly effective adviser and restrainer that reminds man five times a day of what deeds and character are worthy of the divine court, where man must go, what preparations he must make for it, and into what mold he must cast himself.

While this is the primary meaning of the verse, the context also points to a secondary, implicit meaning. This is a sarcasm on those absurdities which the opponents used to utter against Islam and Muslims at all times. This meaning emerges from this verse of Surah Al-Qasas:
(And when they hear ill speech, they turn away from it and say, "For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds.") [Al-Qasas: 55]

This same theme is expressed in another place with the words: (and when they pass by ill speech, they pass as dignified) [Al-Furqan: 72]. In the light of these verses, the verse under discussion also implies that these servants of God, instead of wasting their time in the absurd talk of the opponents, spend their time in the remembrance of their Lord and in contemplating His verses.

"Walladhina hum liz-zakati fa'iloon" (And those who are active in deeds of charity/paying zakah).

After prayer, the second pillar of religion is charity:
After prayer, the second pillar of religion, as we have explained in detail in this book, is Zakah. In Surah Maryam and Surah Al-Anbiya, you have read that regarding every prophet, it has entered: (And he used to enjoin on his people prayer and zakah). The legal form of this charity has differed somewhat in every religion, but it is a comprehensive and well-known word to express spending in the path of Allah, and its status in religion is as the companion of prayer and its primary manifestation. Prayer connects the servant to the Creator, and Zakah links the servant to other servants; and the servant who is connected to both the creation and the Creator on a correct basis is indeed the one worthy of the success of both this world and the Hereafter. Although this Surah is Makkan, and the regular technical system of Zakah in Islam was established later, this word is not in the sense of the technical legal Zakah but in the sense of spending. The opinion of those who have taken it in its verbal noun meaning, i.e., purification of the soul, is incorrect in our view. This word, as we have pointed out, has appeared repeatedly alongside prayer in Makkan surahs as well, and nowhere does it mean purification of the soul, but rather spending in the path of Allah, whose blessings and fruits include purification.

There is an inseparable connection between prayer and charity:
From the perspective of the philosophy of religion, prayer and charity are the two arms of faith, and the common factor between them is gratitude to God. This very feeling of gratitude urges the servant to prayer, which is entirely praise and thanks, and this same feeling of gratitude urges him to spend and sacrifice in the path of God. Pondering from this aspect, it becomes clear that charity is, in reality, a manifestation and aspect of prayer, and there is an inseparable connection between the two.

Furthermore, the spirit of prayer, as we mentioned above, is humility and fear of God; from this aspect, it is something that restrains from every evil and indecency, as made clear above with reference to Surah Al-Ankabut. Consequently, it was stated above that those who have humility in their prayers avoid wrongdoings and vanities. Now, further effects of this prayer are described forward.

Verses 5-7:
Control over carnal desires:
That is, these people keep their carnal desires under control. They only give them freedom where they have a right to do so—meaning, with their wives and those whom their right hands possess [1]. They do not become blinded by desires, behaving completely unbridled and considering violation of every sanctity their right. It is stated that within these limits, this thing is permissible; there is no blame upon anyone for it. This means no one should consider it contrary to piety, religiosity, and fear of God, as is commonly understood under monastic concepts. However, those who exceed this limit are the transgressors of God's limits. Here, only their crime is mentioned; its punishment is not mentioned. Not mentioning the punishment reflects the intensity of anger and aversion.

"Silence conveys meanings that cannot be expressed in words."

It should be remembered here that in the current Western and Westernized society, if there is any restriction on sexual freedom, it is only when coercion and force occur. If this is not the case, then everyone is allowed every kind of freedom.

[1] On the issue of slaves and slave-girls, we have discussed in this book before, and forward in the commentary of Surah An-Noor, a detailed discussion is coming on it.

"Walladhina hum li-amanatihim wa 'ahdihim ra'oon" (And those who are keepers of their trusts and their covenants).

Guarding trusts and covenants:
By "trusts" (amanat) is meant all those trusts which our Lord has entrusted to us in the form of powers and capabilities, duties and responsibilities, or blessings and favors, wealth and children... and those trusts are also included in this which someone has secured with us, or whose fulfillment is due upon us by virtue of rights. Similarly, "covenants" ('ahd) includes all those covenants and pledges which our Lord took from our nature in the world of the unseen, or which He took in this world in the form of His Shariah through the medium of His prophets. Likewise, all those covenants and pledges are included in this which we have made with our Lord through our nature or through the medium of the prophets, or which we have made with any community or individual in this world, whether they were executed verbally and in writing, or are understood and accepted in every civilized society without any writing and agreement. It is stated that these servants of Ours are keepers of all these trusts and all these covenants and pledges. Neither are they treacherous and betraying in their relationship with their Lord, nor are they disloyal and covenant-breaking with His servants.

Within these two words come all those religious, moral, legal, and customary responsibilities whose respect has been desired in every Shariah. Here, this is mentioned as a comprehensive statement after the above matters, and in reality, it is the detail of that summary which is spread throughout the Quran.

A point of the wisdom of religion:
Keep in mind this point of the wisdom of religion: prayer itself is the primary protector of all these trusts and covenants. Prayer itself, as we have explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, is the servant's covenant and pledge with his Lord, which every believing servant renews at least five times a day, and this is also a reminder of all those covenants, pledges, trusts, and duties whose responsibility lies upon the servant. We are discussing these issues in appropriate places in this book, and specifically on the secrets and realities of prayer, we have also written a treatise under the title 'The Reality of Prayer'. Those seeking details may read it.

"Walladhina hum 'ala salawatihim yuhafizoon" (And those who carefully guard their prayers).

If you ponder here, you will realize that the mention of the believers' attributes began with prayer, and concluded upon it. At the beginning, prayer was mentioned from the aspect of its spirit, i.e., humility, and at the end, it was mentioned from the aspect of its guarding, care, and maintenance. This is because those blessings of prayer which have been described are obtained only when the spirit of humility is present in it, and its guarding is constantly maintained. This is a plant of the garden of Paradise which does not grow without complete care; if a little neglect and disregard occurs, it remains fruitless.

Rather, there is fear of its completely drying up. If there is a desire to benefit from its true blessings, protect it from the attacks of the devils, and keep watering it with tears alongside full punctuality of time. Then you will obtain some estimation of what coolness of eyes your Lord has hidden in it!

This fact that prayer alone is the protector of the entire religion is mentioned in places in the Quran. We have referenced all these examples in another place. This is a pointer to that wisdom of religion: from prayer alone all virtues grow, and it alone protects them within its fortress. If prayer does not come into existence, other virtues also cannot come into existence; and if prayer is demolished, the entire garden of religion and morals will be plundered. That is why it is said: whoever loses prayer will lose the rest of religion to a greater degree.

Verses 10-11:
The true heirs of Paradise:
It is stated: "These are the ones who will inherit the Paradise lost by their father (Adam), and then they will have no fear of being expelled from it, as happened to Adam; rather, they will abide in it with their father forever, because they will enter it after defeating their enemy, Satan, forever." Firdaws is among the highest places of Paradise, and this is the portion of those who risk their lives and heads for the supremacy of truth in this world.

[2] Summary of the demands of verses 1-11:
Although we have been pointing to all necessary matters while explaining the verses, since all success and prosperity depends on these very matters, it would be appropriate to refresh them in the mind anew. These matters, with their wise order, are as follows:

1. In the presence of God, all success and prosperity depends on that faith which acquires the status of a mature character inside a person. Mere superficial and hypocritical faith carries no weight with Him.
2. The primary manifestation of this faith is prayer. A faith from which prayer is not born is a dry tree which can serve only as fuel.
3. The true spirit of prayer is humility—the submission of the heart. If prayer is devoid of this humility, it has no useful effect on life.
4. A prayer in which the spirit of humility is present restrains a person from indecencies, wrongdoings, and vain things. In prayer, the servant obtains the consciousness of God's company. This consciousness of company keeps the servant away from all those low things which are not worthy of him.
5. The second manifestation of faith and the companion of prayer is charity—spending in the path of Allah. In the commands of Shariah, these two commands are the greatest. Upon them the entire Shariah depends. Demolishing either of the two is demolishing both. The action taken by Abu Bakr against the rejecters of Zakah was based on this wisdom of religion.
6. Those who accept the rule of God over both their hearts and their wealth never become so blinded in the pursuit of their desires and lusts as to break God's limits and violate the rights and honor of His servants.
7. These people guard all trusts and covenants, whether they relate to God or to His servants.
8. These people guard their prayers, and their prayer, like a city wall, protects their entire religion.
9. Those who are the carriers of these virtues will be the heirs of God's Paradise, and this is the true success.

The details of all these matters will come in the next Surah—Surah An-Noor—which is the completion and complement of this Surah. From that, you will know what light is born in every sphere of life, whether individual, family, or social, when the inner self of man is illuminated with the light of faith, and if he is deprived of it, what kind of darkness spreads in every sphere within and without.

The upcoming theme... Verses 12-22:
Although the glad tiding given to the believers above is a reality, and the warning implicit in it for those deprived of faith is also a reality, what effect could this warning and glad tiding have upon those who did not believe in the Hereafter at all, considering rising again after death highly far-fetched? They considered all these matters merely the product of imagination and mocked them. Keeping their objections in view, forward, attention is directed toward those signs of the soul and the horizons which prove: if anyone ponders over the stages through which the formation of human existence passes, he will not have the slightest doubt that for the God who can do all this, raising them again after they have died and turned to dust is not difficult at all. Similarly, if a person ponders over this universe outside his own existence, he will clearly see that its Creator did not sit detached after creating it; rather, He is supervising and caring for it with great vigilance, care, favor, honor, power, and majesty. So, when He is doing all this, how is it possible that He should leave people completely unbridled, without bringing a Day of Reward and Punishment to reward the good-doers for their good and punish the bad-doers for their evil! This argument has passed in Surah Al-Hajj, verse 5 too. Here, it is stated in some detail, and those aspects have also been highlighted which remained hidden there... In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Verses 12-14:
Man's own existence is a witness to God and the Hereafter:
The description of these gradual stages of human creation, as we pointed out, has also passed in Surah Al-Hajj. There, its introduction was raised with "In kuntum fee raybin mina al-ba'th" (If you are in doubt about the Resurrection), which clarifies that the purpose of its description is the establishing of life after death; likewise, there, within the speech, the words "linubayyina lakum" (that We may make clear to you) also entered, which direct attention to the reality: Allah turned the creation of man into such a sign of the Creator's power, wisdom, and providence that every human being's own existence should serve as such, leaving no need for any external argument regarding the Hereafter. The research of the words of this verse has passed in the commentary of Surah Al-Hajj, and its purpose has also been explained in detail. There is no need for repetition. As far as the creation of man is concerned, these stages are completely known and recognized. Modern science has since unveiled those aspects too to which the Quran merely pointed and left, so that as man's knowledge increases, its secrets will open. To some matters of this kind, we have pointed in places in this book. In short, this secret is known to all: it is a drop of water coming into existence from the essence of clay, which power nurtures stage by stage in such a manner that ultimately it takes the form of Socrates, Hippocrates, Aristotle, or Galen. Now, the question is: is all this happening of its own accord? Is all this gradual development, completion, and training merely the activity of blind and deaf matter? Is all this power, wisdom, providence, and kindness completely without purpose and objective—a game behind which there is no result and end? Is He who can make a man from a drop of water incapable of reviving this man after death? These are the questions in whose answer the fools, even though they may be called by the honorable names of philosophers and scientists, have disagreed. The Quran, to answer such questions, directed man to his own existence, showing: there is no need to go far for their answer. Ponder over the stages of your own creation; you will find the answer to every question.

In "Thumma ansha'nahu khalqan akhar" (then We developed him into another creation), attention is directed to the fact: where a drop of water, and where a fully formed, wise, and mature man—what is the comparison between the two! Yet this miracle of power is present in every existence; so, from the power whose miracle you observe in every existence, why do you consider it far-fetched that He will raise you again? Is that work more difficult than this!

"Fatabarak Allahu ahsanu al-khaliqeen" (So blessed is Allah, the best of creators)—this is the impression and result that should come before one on pondering over all these stages of creation: that Allah, who has displayed these majesties, wisdoms, favors, and blessings within each single existence, is a highly blessed and generous entity who nurtured a drop of lowly water in the pearl-oyster of the womb to bring it out as a lustrous pearl, and deposited such high capabilities inside it that it became the object of the angels' prostration and worthy of God's succession on earth. The meaning is: by pondering over these things, not only does the acknowledgment of God arise inside a person, but also the certainty of His essence being of boundless favor and generosity, and His being a highly wise, powerful, and exalted Creator. Remember here also the style of the Arabic language: the superlative form, particularly when annexed to a plural, sometimes comes independent of the meaning of preference and comparison, merely to express the highest degree of the attribute. For this reason, "ahsanu al-khaliqeen" means that He is not merely a creator, but the best creator. He has not created man just like that, but has created him with the best structure and the best capabilities. Some, due to unfamiliarity with this style, have changed the meaning of "khaliq" (creator), whereas there is no need for any such تصرف. The purpose here is to show: the creation of man is itself witness that he is not the creation of some blind and deaf matter, or some first mover, or cause of causes, nor did his evolution happen of its own accord; rather, he is created by the entity who is completely good and blessed—the Generous God, who is the best creator.

Verses 15-16:
This is the actual result for whose bringing forward all the above stages of creation have been mentioned: that after passing through these stages, inevitably a day comes when you die, and for no one is there any escape from this death. Then, the Day of Resurrection will come, and on that day, you will inevitably be resurrected. There is no reason to consider this resurrection far-fetched; for the God whose majesties you observe within your own existence, what is difficult in raising you again!

"Wa laqad khalaqna fawqakum sab'a tara'iqa wa ma kunna 'ani al-khalqi ghafileen" [17]

Meaning of "Tara'iq":
"Tara'iq" is the plural of "tariqah". "Tara'iq as-surrah" comes in the meaning of stripes/tracks; here, the attribute is spoken to mean the noun, which is common in the Arabic language. That is: seven heavens having stripes/tracks. By this word, pointer is made also to the color and variety of the sky, and to that rain whose mention is coming forward with great care.

"Kunna" (We were) here is for expressing the state, just as it is in "Kana Allahu 'aleeman hakeema". That is: We did not sit detached after creating Our creation, but are constantly caring for and nourishing it with great attention.

Why is there this care of providence?
In the previous verses, the creation of man was mentioned, and the purpose is to direct attention to the possibility of the Return (Ma'ad). Now, pointer is made to the care and arrangement of providence: that God, after creating this world, has not sat independent of its good and evil; rather, He is constantly caring for its nourishment. He built seven variety-filled heavens and He sends down water from the sky and is constantly providing the means of His creation's nourishment.

From water alone is the life of the entire earth, which Allah sends down from the sky, and it is His mercy and providence that He sends it down with a specific measure and amount; otherwise, if He were to open its floodgates at once, no one would be capable of closing them, and this water, instead of being a mercy and blessing, would become a punishment and calamity like the Deluge of Noah. Then, it is His mercy and providence that He lodges this water in the earth, which, like a secure treasury and reservoir, is of use to all creatures. If God willed, He could have made all this water disappear. Not a drop of it could have lodged in this earth. From this, it is known that a single will is active in both the heavens and the earth. If separate wills were active in them, what would have prompted the administrators of heaven to send down water for the residents of earth according to their need and measure, and also care that the reservoir of water remain secured in the earth for times of need. Furthermore, this fact is also known: the entity by whose will all this is happening is a highly kind and generous entity who is caring for this world with great attention.

The context of "ta'kuloon" (you eat) is a clue that after "fawakiha katheeratun" (abundant fruits), a verb like "tatafakkahoon" (you enjoy) or its synonym is assumed as omitted. Examples of this kind of omission have passed before.

That is, from this water alone, Allah made grow for you gardens of palm trees and grapevines, in which are many other fruits for you from which you enjoy, and from these gardens you also obtain your food grains. The meaning is: Allah has not only provided the means of living for you, but has also provided the feast of your taste and mouth. To live, man can live by bread alone, but the Generous Lord has spread different kinds of fruits and table-treats before you. The conception of a good garden among the Arabs, as mentioned in verse 32 of Surah Al-Kahf, is this: it should be a garden of grapes, surrounded by a border of palm trees, and in the middle, plots of different grains and other seasonal fruits. The actual fruits in their view were indeed the palm and the grape, but other fruits and some food grains also grew. To these, the words "fawakiha katheeratun" and "minha ta'kuloon" point.

By "shajarah" (tree) is meant the olive tree. Although its name is not taken, the attributes mentioned of it themselves tell that it refers to the olive. Mount Sinai was the specific region of its production. Its oil was highly popular among the Arabs, and among the People of the Book, it was accorded not only the status of a dietary oil but also a kind of religious sanctity. "Sibgh" means condiment. Like butter, this was a delicious and nutritious condiment. Here, its indefinite form is proof of its goodness. That is: "sibghin lil-akileen" (a good condiment for those who eat). This is the description of a further goodness of it along with its other benefits.

"'Ibrah" means, as explained under Al-Imran 13, to cross over from one reality to another reality and obtain a lesson from it. Its indefinite form is for highlighting its status. The meaning is: the lesson and advice present in the things mentioned above are indeed there; if you ponder, there is also a great lesson for you inside your beasts.

The second object of "nusqeekum" (We give you drink) is not expressed here. In another place, it is expressed with the words: (pure milk, agreeable to those who drink) [An-Nahl: 66]. Similarly, "mimma fee butooniha" (from what is in their bellies) is clarified in another place with the words: (from between excretion and blood) [An-Nahl: 66]. If you open these omissions, the meaning will be: We give you to drink from between the excretion and blood inside the bellies of these beasts completely pure and pleasant milk.

"Wa lakum feeha manafi'u katheeratun wa minha ta'kuloon"—along with their specific benefit, this has also directed attention to their other benefits and profits: that not only their milk but their every single thing is beneficial for you. They are of use in your carriage and load-carrying, and they are helpers in your cultivation. Their meat, skin, and their wool—everything is useful for you. To the extent that even their dung serves as manure for your fields, and they also hold the status of a food reservoir for you—you eat their fresh meat and also dry their meat to preserve it for your travel needs, and some among them are those whose primary importance is in terms of their dietary aspect.

"Wa 'alayha wa 'ala al-fulki tuhmaloon" (And upon them and upon ships you are carried).

Within "tuhmaloon" (you are carried) are included both riding and load-carrying, and since the life of the Arabs was generally nomadic, moving constantly from one place to another with the seasons, for this reason, both these issues held great importance for them. Allah created the camel for this need of theirs, which is the ship of their desert. Consequently, the mention of it and the mention of the ship are made together, showing: We made the arrangement of the ship for your desert too and for the sea too.

Pointer to the actual reality:
Keep in view the actual reality: the purpose of mentioning these things is to obtain a lesson: has the Lord who cared for our every single need with this detail created us without any purpose, sitting detached? Does this providence of His not impose any duty and responsibility upon us? And does its inevitable demand not require Him to bring a day in which we are questioned regarding these responsibilities—those who fulfilled them receive the reward, and those who considered this world a child's play, spending their entire lives in vanity, suffer its punishment! It is obvious: the testimony of intellect and nature is in favor of this very latter aspect. Those who, after receiving everything from the Generous, do not recognize His right or sing the praises of others besides Him are ungrateful, and it is necessary that they face the end of this ungratefulness.

The upcoming theme... Verses 23-50:
Forward, there is a brief reference to the history of the prophets (peace be upon them) and their deniers, and the purpose of it is to clarify that reality in the light of history which is under discussion here. That is: the success of the believers and the end of those who deny the truth. Within this, pointer is also made to the Divine Sunnah: that although the end of those who deny the messengers is indeed eternal loss, Allah also tests the people of truth in this world. For the demand of this trial, the people of falsehood are also given respite for a period. They consider this respite as their success, whereas this thing is not their success but becomes a death-trap for them.

The reference to the prophets (peace be upon them) and their deniers has also passed in verses 42-45 of the previous Surah, but very briefly. In this, there is detail, but the mention by name among the early prophets is only of Hazrat Nuh (Noah), and among the later prophets, of Hazrat Musa, Hazrat Haroon (Aaron), and Hazrat Masih (Jesus) (peace be upon them). The other prophets are pointed to without taking their names... In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

A beautiful transition:
Here is a highly beautiful and eloquent transition (takhlees), which we call "greez" in the poetic terminology. The argument from the testimonies of providence on reward and punishment concluded upon: "wa 'alayha wa 'ala al-fulki tuhmaloon" (and upon them and upon ships you are carried). Now forward, when the chain of historical testimonies began, first of all, the event of Hazrat Nuh was taken, who is also the preface of the messengers' stories in terms of historical precedence, and specifically, the ship was made by Allah as the medium of his and his companions' salvation. After the mention of the ship, the mention of this event of the ship came in such a manner as if one thing arose from another.

The story of Hazrat Nuh from a specific aspect:
In both the Torah and the Quran, the history of the messengers begins from Hazrat Nuh alone. Different aspects of his story have been mentioned in the previous surahs. Here, the purpose with which this story is mentioned, as we have pointed out, is: those who assume that God, after creating this world, has sat detached from it are completely mistaken. God has always made arrangement for its material nourishment and also sent His messengers for its spiritual and moral reform. Those who denied these messengers, God destroyed them after the completion of proof, and those who followed them, He granted them salvation and success. This dealing of God with this world is proof: ultimately, He will also bring a day in which He will decide between the good-doers and the bad-doers with justice, and give everyone reward or punishment according to their actions.

After "afala tattaqoon" (will you not then fear?), its object is omitted. That is: do you not fear the anger and wrath of God, that you associate others in His godship and worship them? This is open rebellion, for which you are worthy of His punishment at every moment.

Opposition to Hazrat Nuh from the leaders of the nation:
By "mula'" is meant, as explained under Al-Baqarah 246, the chiefs, nobles, and leaders of the nation. In opposing the call of truth, always these have been in the forefront, because being the heads of the false system of their time, they consider the success of the messenger as their own death. Consequently, when they see that the call of the messenger is influencing the sound-natured people, they make various kinds of verbal fabrications to erase its effect and keep their common folk in their grip. First of all, they attack his intention and truthfulness: "This claim of this man that he is an emissary of God is merely a lie and fabrication! He fabricated this lie only to establish his leadership over you through the clout of being a divine emissary." Ponder over the eloquence of "yatafaddalu 'alaykum" (he wishes to assert superiority over you). It did not say "he wants to snatch our leadership" but "he wants to establish his authority over you." The venomous material present in this zahar-alood phrase to instigate the simple-minded common folk is not hidden from those of insight.

An excuse for opposition:
"Wa law sha'a Allahu la-anzala mala'ikatan"—this is the intellectual argument of these wiseacres mentioned to make their common folk foolish, saying: "First of all, there is no need for any prophet and messenger for our guidance; we ourselves are sufficient for our guidance; and even if hypothetically God were to send someone as a prophet and messenger, He would have chosen the angels for this specific work. After all, what is the meaning of making a human being like ourselves a prophet and messenger!"

"Ma sami'na bihadha fee aba'ina al-awwaleen"—meaning, further calamity is: what this man presents is entirely innovation, error, and completely contrary to the way of our ancestors. We have never heard such things in the traditions of our ancestors. This same theme is stated in verse 68 of this Surah: (Then have they not reflected over the Word, or has there come to them that which had not come to their forefathers?) [Al-Mu'minun: 68]. In Surah Al-Qasas, it is said: (This is not but invented magic, and we have not heard of this among our forefathers.) [Al-Qasas: 36]. In another place, this theme is: (We have not heard of this in the latest religion) [Sad: 7].

Accusing the messenger of mental disorder:
That is, these leaders also tried to convince their common folk: "This man who claims that some angel comes to him from God with revelation—do not be awed by his words. This is merely a kind of mental disorder because of which he suffers from a kind of whisper which he assumes to be heavenly revelation." "Fatarabbasoo bihi hatta heenin"—meaning, there is no basis for these words of this man. Do not fall into any fear of this future punishment he is warning you of. All these are his whispers. Very soon, they will fly in the wind. Exactly this kind of statement was made by the leaders of Quraysh regarding the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), which is referenced in Surah At-Toor: (Or do they say, "A poet for whom we await a misfortune of time"?) [At-Tur: 30]. The Quraysh meant: let no one be mesmerized by his spellbinding discourse to fall into the misunderstanding that there is any basis for his words and now the decision of the future is in his hands; this is merely poetic imagination and a temporary heat of some days. Very soon, it will be finished. Just as many of our poets flew away singing their songs, similarly one day he too will fly away. It should be kept in mind: when the leaders of the time begin to sense that the public is accepting a person as a leader or savior of the future, they become highly anxious about it. Regarding him, they try to convince their followers that all this magic of his is temporary.

"Qala rabbi ansurnee bima kadhdhaboon" (He said, "My Lord, support me because they have denied me").

Hazrat Nuh's prayer for the manifestation of the promise of punishment:
That is, when the matter reached this limit, Hazrat Nuh made this prayer: "O Lord! Now decide between me and my nation, and help me by fulfilling that promise of punishment of which I warned them night and day, but they turned a deaf ear and denied this punishment." The mention of Hazrat Nuh's prayer will come in detail in Surah Nuh. Here, immediately after this prayer, in the subsequent verse, the direction for Hazrat Nuh to make the ship and avoid the punishment was given, which is a clear clue: this prayer of Hazrat Nuh was for the manifestation of the promise of punishment. Forward in this connection, this same prayer is narrated from other prophets too. See verse 39. In reply to it, it was assured by Allah: (He said, "In a little [time] they will surely become regretful." So the shriek seized them) [Al-Mu'minun: 40-41]. It should be kept in mind: in the struggle between the prophets (peace be upon them) and their opponents, the final stage always came when, of the punishment they warned their nation, the rebels demanded that very punishment, and when, under the Divine Sunnah of respite, some delay occurred, the deniers announced with great defiance: "Since you could not bring that punishment of which you have been warning us night and day, for this reason, you too are a liar and this warning of punishment is also a lie." This is the very stage in which the prophets (peace be upon them) prayed: (Our Lord, decide between us and our people in truth, and You are the best of those who decide.) [Al-A'raf: 89]. In this stage, Hazrat Nuh also made this prayer, and its meaning is what we have explained. The author of 'Kashshaf' has taken this meaning of the verse: "Help me by executing what You promised them of the punishment, which they denied me in." This is the very thing regarding which his nation denied him.

Explanation of some summaries:
This verse, rather the entire story of Hazrat Nuh, has passed in Surah Hud. For this reason, here we will content ourselves with pointers. If the detail of anything is required, see there. "Bi-a'yunina wa wahyina"—meaning, make this ship under Our care and Our directions. From the Torah, it is known that its entire design, with the details of its parts, was told by Allah to Hazrat Nuh through His revelation, so that it might be completely suitable for that great mission which was in view. By "amr" (command) is meant the command of punishment. "Fara at-tannoor" is an idiom of this kind as are "humiya al-watees", etc. In determining the meaning of these idioms, the words are not considered, but only that meaning is considered for which they are used. "Fara at-tannoor" is an expression for the deluge pouring out and boiling. In "min kullin zawjayni ithnayn", the word "kull" (every), as we have explained in another place, is used in terms of contextual implication. That is: of those animals which are directly related to the economic need of man. "Ithnayn" (two) is as an explanation of "zawjayn" (pairs)—meaning, of every thing, just keep a male and a female, just two, do not worry about keeping more than this. The explanation of the word "Ahl" has passed in Surah Al-Anbiya: that in it are included a person's household along with his followers and companions. It is proved from other places of the Quran: some people of his nation believed in Hazrat Nuh, though their number was very small. By "illa man sabaqa 'alayhi al-qawlu" (except those against whom the Word has preceded) is meant those for whom, after the completion of proof, under the Divine Sunnah, destruction has now been decreed. Among them, the most prominent, as clear from other places of the Quran, was Hazrat Nuh's own son; it is said: "These people will not be with you in this ship." "Wa la tukhatibnee fee alladhina dalamoo"—by "dalamoo" is meant "dalamoo anfusahum" (they wronged themselves); i.e., those who, by persisting in denying you, invited their own doom. It is said: "Let it not be that when the waves of divine wrath surround these wrongdoers, the feeling of kindness for them arises inside you, and you begin to pray to Us for their salvation. After the arrival of punishment, this stage will pass. Then, no prayer of yours regarding them will be heard. Then, every single one of them must drown." This aspect of "wa la tukhatibnee..." should specifically remain in view: when the divine punishment arrives, its nature is such that even in them, the feeling of kindness for the punished can arise for whose support and help it manifests, but then no one's prayer or intercession is of any use in the presence of Allah.

Verses 28-29:
Hazrat Nuh's migration and the prayer appropriate for migration:
Hazrat Nuh and his companions boarding the ship was a migration of the same kind from their nation as the migration other messengers had to do; for this reason, they were taught a prayer appropriate for the state of migration: that after boarding, make this prayer: "Praise be to Allah who saved us from the wrongdoing people," and along with it, make this prayer too: "My Lord, land me at a blessed landing-place, and You are the best of those who bring to land." At the time of migration, prayers of this theme are narrated from other prophets too. Our Prophet too made a prayer of this kind at the time of migration: (My Lord, cause me to enter a sound entrance and to exit a sound exit and grant me from Yourself a supporting authority) [Al-Isra: 80]. Since this prayer is taught by Allah, inside the style of prayer, this is also glad tidings of future victory and success. The meaning is: "Now your Lord has delivered you from the environment of the impure and insolent. Now He will land you like an excellent host, and where He lands you, He will make that land blessed for you; you will flourish and multiply, and from these few individuals and little means of living, this world will be populated and made active anew."

Pointer to the actual purpose of the story:
This is a pointer to the actual purpose for which this story was narrated. The meaning is: inside this story, for those who ponder, are many realities. The most prominent reality from it is: God, after creating this world, has not sat detached from it; rather, He sent His messengers for the reform and guidance of creation. The second reality clarified is: those who persist in opposing and denying the messenger are given respite by Allah for a specific limit, but ultimately God grasps them, and when He grasps, no one can deliver them. The third reality clarified is: in the struggle between truth and falsehood, victory and success are obtained by the messenger and his companions. However, before this victory and success, they have to pass through a period of trial. By "wa in kunna lamubtaleen" (indeed, We were testing), pointer is made to this Divine Sunnah. The fourth reality clarified is: just as the call of the messengers has always been one, similarly the style of opposition of their opponents has always been one; for this reason, every messenger's life is an independent lesson for other messengers, and every nation's story is an independent lesson for other nations.

"Thumma ansha'na min ba'dihim qarnan akhareen" (Then We produced after them another generation).

Pointer to 'Ad and Thamud:
"Qarn", as explained in its place, comes in the meaning of a century too, and by it is meant a generation of people, nation, and community too. The meaning is: after destroying the people of Noah, We raised other nations from the offspring of Noah and his companions of the ship. This is a pointer to 'Ad and Thamud. In Surah Al-A'raf, the mention of 'Ad has passed as the successor of the people of Noah, and of Thamud as the successor of 'Ad.

Pointer to Hazrat Hud and Hazrat Salih:
This is a pointer to those messengers who were sent to 'Ad and Thamud—meaning, Hazrat Hud and Hazrat Salih, whose stories have passed in Surah Hud and Surah Al-A'raf. Before "an i'budoo Allah", a noun is assumed as omitted. That is: We sent them with this message: "O people! Worship Allah, you have no other deity besides Him." This call is exactly the same call Hazrat Nuh gave, whose mention has passed above.

"Rasoolan minhum"—meaning, this messenger too was of these nations. He was no angel or jinn. It is a very great favor of Allah that He sent a messenger to every nation from among themselves. We have pointed to the different aspects of this favor in its place.

The insolence of the arrogant:
The explanation of "mula'" has passed under verse 24. Here, some such attributes of these arrogant ones are mentioned from which light is shed on the actual cause of their rebellion and arrogance. Among them, the first thing is: these people were deniers and deniers of the Hereafter and the presence before God in the Hereafter. The second thing is: the luxury and prosperity they obtained in the world became a trial for them. They assumed that their worldly successes were proof of the correctness of their action and belief; in their assumption, the Hereafter first of all does not exist, and even if it does, what they have obtained here—there is no reason why they should remain deprived of it in the Hereafter. Because of this wealth and prosperity, they were not ready to accept anyone's superiority over themselves, and that too the superiority of a person who did not possess wealth and status like them! Over this pride of theirs, they tried to put a veil: "If God were to send someone for our guidance, He would have sent some superhuman entity. What is the meaning of making a human being like ourselves a messenger!"

Emotional weapon to instigate the common folk:
This is an emotional weapon they used to instigate their common folk: "If you obey a human being like yourselves, accepting him as God's messenger and placing the collar of obedience on your neck, you will ruin yourselves both in terms of religion and the world. Who knows into what ditch he will throw you and where he will destroy you, leaving you unable to find water!" The meaning is: "Before taking such a destructive step, think well. We explain to you the good and bad well!"

Verses 35-36:
The context and meaning of "Hayhat":
"Ya'idukum" (he promises you) here is in the meaning of warning, just as it is in "Ash-shaytanu ya'idukumu al-faqra" [Al-Baqarah: 268]—meaning, Satan threatens you with poverty. "Hayhat" is a verbal noun. It is used in different forms in Arabic, and is spoken when something is to be expressed as highly far-fetched and impossible. In the case of repetition, the meaning of emphasis and intensity is born inside it. The interrogation here is in the meaning of rejection.

That is: they say with great surprise and wonder: "Does this man warn you that when you die and turn to dust and bones, you will be brought out again alive from the graves! This warning you are being warned of is highly far-fetched and highly impossible!" The meaning is: "Do not be awed by these imaginary words of this man to fall into his trap."

The loss of intellect of the deniers of Resurrection:
That is: "Life is only our life of this world. Here we die and here we live. There is no possibility of being raised after death." It should be kept in mind: whether the old deniers of Resurrection or the new, both denied the Resurrection not on the basis of any argument, but merely on the basis of its far-fetchedness; whereas things thousands and millions of times more far-fetched than this they observe with their own eyes and believe in them. The Quran has expressed surprise in places at this loss of intellect of the people.

The above words indeed were said by these wiseacres to make their common folk averse and incited, and then they expressed their wisdom and insight in these words: "If anyone, out of his simplicity, believes this man to be a messenger, let them. In our view, he has fabricated this lie against God that God has sent him as His messenger. For this reason, we are not going to believe this claim of his under any circumstances."
This verse has passed above, and there its complete explanation has passed.

In reply to the mentioned prayer of the Prophet, Allah assured him: "Now there is no much delay in the manifestation of the promise of Allah; on this arrogance of theirs, these people will very soon become regretful."

Meaning of "Sayhah":
"Sayhah" means a shriek/blast, and in the Quran, it is used in places for the expression of divine punishment, regardless of the form in which that punishment manifested. Restricting it specifically to the punishment of a loud voice is incorrect in our view.

Meaning of "Haqq":
By "haqq" (truth) here is meant that true promise which, in the case of denial, was inevitably to manifest in the form of punishment, of which the Prophet had warned them.

Meaning of "Ghuthâ'":
The word "ghuthâ'" is used in two different meanings in the Holy Quran and Arabic speech. If God wills, we will discuss it in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-A'la. Here, it is in the meaning of ruined and swept-away dry grass and debris.

The meaning is: after this prayer of the Prophet, the punishment of God arrived upon them with that actual reality which people were considering a mere empty threat, and We turned them completely into swept-away debris.

"Fabu'dan lil-qawmi ad-dalimeena" is a phrase of curse and rejection. That is: "rejection be upon such insolent ones!" This is the spiritual expression of that punishment. Divine punishment, in reality, is the curse of God.

Pointer to the subsequent prophets:
In verse 31 above, the word "qarn" has entered, and here "quroon" is the plural. This is a pointer to the fact: after that, gradually, multiple nations arose from the offspring of Hazrat Nuh, and for the guidance and reform of each of them, God sent His messengers. Although the names of all these messengers are mentioned neither in the Torah nor in the Quran, in both the Quran and the Torah, this pointer is present: God sent His messengers to all nations. In the Quran, this is stated in highly clear words, but the Jews, since they have always been in pursuit of erasing the history of other prophets except the prophets of their specific family, for this reason, this is not mentioned in clear words in the Torah. However, pointers are present in it too.

The measure of the determined term of nations, and warning to the Quraysh:
In my view, in accordance with the well-known rule of the Arabic language, before the imperfect verb, a past-tense helping verb is assumed as omitted, and between the above verse and this verse, based on contextual clarity, there is also a connection. If you open the omission and fill the connection, the meaning of the verse will be: "We sent Our messengers to those nations, but they denied them, whose result ultimately was: every nation, after completing its given respite, met its end; neither did punishment come upon any nation before its determined term was completed, nor was any nation given a single minute of respite after the determined term was completed." Regarding the term of the destruction of nations, we have explained in another place: Allah has prescribed this term by measuring it with the scale of the moral decline of nations, and it is known to God alone when any nation reaches that limit of moral decline when its existence must be purified from God's earth. This is a warning to the Quraysh: "Do not make haste for the punishment from your messenger. When your cup is full, you too will be destroyed. Neither because of your making haste will God send punishment upon you before the time, nor will He give a single second of respite after the time is completed." The Divine Sunnah regarding nations has always been this, and in accordance with this Sunnah, the deal will be made with you too.

Meaning of "Tatra":
The origin of "tatra" is "watra". In accordance with the well-known rule of Arabic, the 'wa' has changed to 'ta'. "Ja'a al-qawmu tatra" means the people came in succession, one after another.

With the increase in the number of nations, the care for their reform also increased:
Gradually, in the measure of the increase in the number of nations, Allah also increased the arrangement and care for their guidance and reform in the same pace. To the extent that in a single era, separate messengers came to separate nations. Although details are not found in histories, the existence of Hazrat Ibrahim and Hazrat Lot in one era is known from the Quran. Similarly, Hazrat Musa, Hazrat Haroon, and Hazrat Shu'ayb are messengers of a single era. This arrangement is clear proof: God has never been indifferent to the reform of His creation. But to whichever nation its messenger came, it denied him, whose result ultimately was: one after another, God threw every nation into the same ditch of destruction into which He threw its predecessor nation. "Wa ja'alnahum ahadeetha"—meaning, then it happened that those who were highly proud of their power, majesty, clout, and rise became merely a tale of the past. "Fabu'dan liqawmin la yu'minoon"—this, as passed in verse 41, is a phrase of curse and rejection, and from the context and position of this phrase, it is understood: although the history of these nations has become very old, the anger of God on their lack of faith is still in force, and even today, with their mention, God curses them.

Meaning of "Ayat" and "Sultan Mubeen":
After pointing briefly to all the messengers after Hazrat Nuh, this is the mention of those messengers whose detailed history is present in the scrolls of the Torah and Gospel. It is said: "We sent Moses and his brother Aaron with Our signs and a clear authority to Pharaoh and his chiefs." By "ayat" (signs) is meant, in my view, those common miracles which manifested through the hands of Hazrat Musa for the warning of the Egyptians. Their details are present in the Torah too, and we have given references to them in this book too. By "sultan mubeen" (clear authority), in my view, is meant the miracle of the staff. This is the mention of the specific after the general. This miracle held, in reality, the status of a clear proof and authority; for this reason, it was expressed as "sultan mubeen". Through this miracle, Pharaoh and his courtiers suffered a highly humiliating defeat in the presence of their nation and all their expert magicians. In some places in the Quran, it is expressed as "bayyinah" (clear proof)—meaning, a highly clear argument. The word "sultan" has also been used in Surah Ar-Rahman, verse 33 "la tanfudhoona illa bisultanin", in the meaning of divine authority and decree. From this perspective too, this word is highly appropriate for the miracle of the staff, because its status in the hand of Musa was indeed as a divine decree.

Verses 46-48:
The specific characteristic of the Egyptians' national temperament:
That is: "They too adopted the same method of arrogance which has always been of the deniers of the messengers." "Wa kanoo qawman 'aleen"—this is a pointer to the specific characteristic of their national temperament: that this arrogance was not the result of some temporary national heat or excitement; rather, they were indeed rebellious and proud people. Because of this temperament of the Egyptians, when Allah sent Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) to Pharaoh, He said: (Go to Pharaoh, indeed he has transgressed) [Ta-Ha: 24], and this fact too we have mentioned in its place: at the very first stage, the reason why He granted the miracle of the staff to Hazrat Musa was: their encounter was with hardcore rebels. For the suppression of these rebels, it was necessary for Hazrat Musa to be armed. "Faqaloo anu'minu libasharayni mithlina wa qawmuhuma lana 'abidoon"—this is the explanation of their arrogance: they considered themselves superior to the truth, saying: "How can we believe in two human beings like ourselves and accept their superiority over us, particularly when their nation is doing our service and slavery!" "Fakadhdhaboohuma fakanoo mina al-muhlakeen"—but the result was: falling into this pride, they denied their messengers and fell into the very ditch in which many of their counterparts before them had fallen and been destroyed.

The favor upon Hazrat Musa and his nation:
After the suppression of Pharaoh and his chiefs, this is the favor Allah did upon Hazrat Musa and his nation: Pharaoh and his courtiers indeed remained in this pride as to how they should believe in a person who is an individual of a nation doing their slavery, but Allah favored that very person and his nation with His Book, so that they might obtain guidance from it in the stages of life. It should be kept in mind: by "Al-Kitab" (The Book) here, there is no room to mean anything except the Torah, and regarding the Torah, it is known that it was granted to Hazrat Musa after the migration from Egypt. "Yahtadoon" (they may be guided) is in the plural form, used in terms of the nation, whose mention has passed in the Egyptians' statement "wa qawmuhuma lana 'abidoon".

Meaning of "Rabwah", "Dhat Qarar", and "Ma'een"; brief pointer to the story of Hazrat Maryam and Hazrat Masih:
"Rabwah" means high and even land. "Dhat qarar" means peaceful, and "ma'een" means spring.

This is a pointer to the time of birth of our master Jesus. When Hazrat Maryam was given the unseen direction to sit under a tree in a separate place. This place was high and peaceful. Here, there was also a fruit-bearing palm tree, and beneath it was a clean, transparent, sweet spring. In this delicate stage of her life, Hazrat Maryam was in intense need of a peaceful corner of solitude, to remain safe from the gibes and criticisms of the public. Allah, by His special favor and mercy, provided a peaceful corner of comfort for her. All this detail has passed in Surah Maryam. There we have pointed to the location and place of this "rabwah" as well, and also explained this event being a great sign. Since these details had passed in the previous surahs, the Quran merely pointed here.

"So read the detailed story yourself from this summary."

The upcoming theme... Verses 51-67:
Forward, it is clarified: Allah gave one single religion to all these messengers, but their nations broke this religion into pieces, and now whichever has what, is pleased in it, and is not ready to hear and understand anything contrary to it. After that, comfort is given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Give them some more days and opportunity. They are pleased with their worldly successes and assume they are hitting great targets, whereas they are merely providing the means of their own destruction, but they do not realize it. The true victories, in reality, are being obtained by those who today fear God and believe in His verses. Indeed, these are the ones who are to win the game. As for these self-deluded people, soon that day is coming when they will beat their heads on their wretchedness, but no one will listen to them." In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

The common direction to all messengers:
This is a reference to the direction Allah gave to all His messengers: "Eat of the pure things and do righteous deeds; indeed, of what you do, I am well-aware." The purpose of referencing this is to show: Allah indeed gave this direction to His messengers, they acted upon it, and they invited their respective nations to it, but their nations, of their own accord, under their polytheistic delusions, made pure things unlawful and impure things lawful, and left the highway shown by their messengers to adopt the path of bad deeds, disbelief, and disobedience.

Within "Innee bima ta'maloona 'aleem" (Indeed, I am aware of what you do), there is both warning and comfort. That is: if you do good deeds, it will be in My knowledge, and I will give its full reward; and if you commit evil, it will be in My knowledge, and I will surely give its punishment.

Verses 52-53:
The attitude of the nations contrary to the common call of all prophets:
The call of all these prophets has been one, and all of them are one single nation. They came with one single religion and tried to make people one single nation, and I am the Lord of all of you. That is, the actual reality is indeed this, but the nations, through their mutual obstinacy, broke their single religion into pieces, and now the state is: whichever has what, is pleased in it. This exact same thing, after referencing the call of all prophets, is stated in Surah Al-Anbiya thus: (Indeed, this nation of yours is one nation, and I am your Lord, so worship Me. But they threw their affair into pieces among themselves) [Al-Anbiya: 92-93].

This same theme, with more detail, has passed in Surah Al-Baqarah:
(Mankind was [of] one religion [before they deviated]; then Allah sent the prophets as bringers of good tidings and warners and sent down with them the Scripture in truth to judge between the people concerning that in which they differed. And none differed over the Scripture except those who were given it—after the clear proofs came to them—out of jealous animosity among themselves.) [Al-Baqarah: 213]

The position of the Quran regarding the prophets and their nations:
Understand well the position of the Quran regarding the prophets and their nations. Although its explanation has passed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah and Surah Al-Anbiya, due to not understanding it well, people have fallen into severe misunderstandings; for this reason, we clarify the actual reality again.

The Quran does not accept that the prophets and messengers who came into the world came with different calls, starting different nations; rather, its claim is: every prophet struggled to establish one single nation—the Muslim nation. When the nations created corruption in this religion, Allah sent other prophets and messengers to reform this corruption. These prophets and messengers did not present anything separate from the original religion, but only spent all their strength on establishing the original religion, and if the wisdom of Allah demanded, they highlighted further requirements of this religion. The Quran is the final link of this blessed chain. It has presented the original religion, whose call every prophet from Adam and Nuh until Hazrat Masih gave, completely clearly, in its original form, and those aspects which were still lacking completion, and whose completion was postponed to the final mission, in accordance with the prophecies of the previous prophets, it completed. In its reality, this is the original religion of all creeds and nations. But the nations, due to their narrow-mindedness and bias, opposed it, and now whichever has what, no matter how محرف and incomplete, is rigid upon it and pleased in it... It should be kept in mind: this is said to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), showing: those who are so wretched as not to want to benefit from their own lost treasury—how far will you grieve for them!

"Fadharhum fee ghamratihim hatta heenin" (So leave them in their confusion for a time).

"Ghamrah" here means the intoxication of heedlessness. Addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), He said: "When you shook them so much, but they are not ready to change, leave them to their state. They will very soon see the end of this intoxication of theirs." "Hatta heen" (until a time) refers to that term of respite which was prescribed for them, and whose end was now drawing near.

Verses 55-56:
Warning to the worldly-intoxicated:
"Mal wa baneen" (wealth and children) is a comprehensive expression of worldly prosperity. The meaning is: the actual cause of the intoxication of these intoxicated ones is: the worldly prosperities We have given them are not letting them awaken. They assume: this luxury they possess is proof of the success of their life, and these successes and victories will constantly increase; for this reason, they should not disturb their luxury on being warned by some warner. He said: "If they are assuming so, they are assuming very wrongly. These things they are considering successes are not successes; rather, this is the trap of God's entrapment, falling into which they will never be able to escape; but due to their simpletonness, they do not realize this reality."

Verses 57-61:
Attributes of those who win the game:
Take a look back at the initial ten verses of the Surah. Those believers who were given the glad tidings of success there, their mention is made again with slightly different words: "Indeed, those who possess these attributes are hastening in the path of true successes, and indeed these are the ones who will reach their destination." The following attributes of these people are counted here, and these are the attributes of those Muslims who, in that period, were turning their faces from all worldly beloveds to risk their lives for seeking the pleasure of Allah.

Their first attribute is: they are constantly fearful and trembling with the fear of their Lord. The testimony of this is given by their prayers. Consequently, it was said above: (Those who are humble in their prayers).

Their second attribute is: "walladhina hum bi-ayati rabbihim yu'minoon"—these do not mock the verses of God like the arrogant; rather, when the Messenger of Allah recites these verses to them, they say, "We believe and testify."

Their third attribute is: "walladhina hum birabbihim la yushrikoon"—all their reliance is upon their Lord; they do not associate any partner with Him.

Their fourth attribute is: "walladhina yu'toona ma ataw wa quloobuhum wajilatun annahum ila rabbihim raji'oon"—they spend in God's path, and whatever they spend, they spend not for show and pride, but for the pleasure of God, knowing they have to face Him one day. In my view, "annahum ila rabbihim raji'oon" is as an explanation of "wajilah" (fearful)—this has explained that fear which is settled in their hearts.

"Ola'ika yusari'oona fee al-khayrati wa hum laha sabiqoon"—He said: "Indeed, these are the ones who are hastening in the path of goodnesses and successes, and they will get the fruits of these virtues, not those who are considering Our entrapment as their victories, and are constantly advancing toward the pit of their destruction with closed eyes." This verse is in contrast to verse 56 above, and is the further explanation of the initial theme "Qad aflaha al-mu'minoon...". Here, also keep in view the difference in the style of expression of both verses: above, He said: "nusari'u lahum fee al-khayrati" (these intoxicated with worldly luxury are in the delusion that in their wealth and children, We are adding for them to hasten for them goodnesses), and regarding these believers, He said: "ola'ika yusari'oona fee al-khayrati" (indeed, these are the ones who are hastening in the path of goodnesses, and they will win their game). This difference in style clarifies: whatever man gets in this world, he gets only by the giving of God, and its status is of trial, not of reward. However, in the Hereafter, whatever man gets, it will be the fruit of his own effort and the reward of his own labor; so, whomever wants to compete, let him do in this field, not in that field whose every single thing is merely temporary and trial-based.

A glad tiding to the believers:
This, as a parenthetical clause, is a glad tiding and comfort for the believers, of the same kind as passed in the final verse of Surah Al-Baqarah. That is: "Although this trial is severe, let the believers remain assured: We do not put those who compete in Our path into any trial which is beyond their strength; and let them also remain assured: whatever hardship they suffer in Our path, whether small or great, is recorded with Us in a register; that register, on the Day of Reward of Actions, will present the complete record of everyone's actions, completely correctly, and everyone will receive the reward of their actions according to it, without the slightest injustice to anyone."

Verses 63-64:
The end of the wealthy and their followers:
This turn of speech relates to that delusion of the arrogant mentioned above—meaning, they are making their prosperity and comfort the proof of their good action and good end. There, only pointer was made to their delusion, and the speech turned to the mention of the believers. Now, that same connection of speech returned. He said: "The hearts of these people are in veils of heedlessness from this warning which God's Messenger is warning them of, and they will continue in their intoxication in this manner. The actions of the believers mentioned in verses 57-60 have no attraction for them. Their interest is in other actions, and they will remain absorbed in them, until when God's punishment grasps them, they will cry out, but at that time, their crying will be completely useless."

"Ja'ara-yaj'aru" means to humiliate oneself and cry out, and "mutafeen" means the wealthy and comfortable. The divine punishment that comes as a result of the messenger's denial comes principally for the wealthy alone, because the primary check on the messenger's warning is from them. For this reason, He said: "We grasped their comfortable ones with the punishment," but along with them, those are also included in its wave who do not belong to the class of the wealthy but, due to their wretchedness of action, become their tools; for this reason, just as we have with wheat, they too are ground with their patrons.

Verses 65-66:
That is, at that time, the answer to their crying from Our side will be: "Now, the time of crying and humiliating before Us has passed. Its opportunity was before, but in that time, your arrogance was such: when Our warning verses were recited to you, you turned on your heels, not ready to hear any word of Our Messenger—so now, taste the consequence of your obstinacy! Now, do not expect any good for yourselves from Our side, and no other will be able to help you against Us today." In the story of Pharaoh too, the exact same thing is mentioned. When he was caught in the wave of the deluge, he cried: "I believe in the Lord of Moses and Aaron." But this acknowledgment of faith of his was not accepted. Rather, he was answered: "Now you believe! While you transgressed before." We have pointed to the fact: the speech can be in words and also the expression of the state's situation.

"Mustakbireena bihi samiran tahjuroon" (In arrogance regarding it, conversing by night, speaking evil).

The structure and interpretation of verse 67:
In the structure and interpretation of this verse, our commentators have faced great anxiety. To quote their statements and criticize them would cause great length; for this reason, we present only our view.

After "istikbar" (arrogance), the preposition "Bi" is a clue that this word here contains the meaning of "istihza'" (mockery). Examples of this have passed before.

The referent of the pronoun "bihi" has been accepted by a group of commentators as the word "ayat" (verses) mentioned in the above verse, and they have taken it, by way of interpretation, in the meaning of reminder and Book. Although this is not contrary to Arabic, and "ayat", "kitab", "dhikr", and "rasool" are used as exchangeable words in the Quran, in our view, there is no need for this. Often, the referent of the pronoun or the pointed-to of the demonstrative pronoun is not mentioned in words, but is understood from the context of speech, and the clue determines it. Parallels of this are present in both the Quran and Arabic speech. Many examples of this have passed in this book, and in the surahs of the last group, highly eloquent examples of this will come. For this reason, in our view, the referent of this pronoun is the Messenger, whose denial and mockery are under discussion in this Surah, and the recitation of whose verses is mentioned in "qad kanat ayatee tutla 'alaykum".

"Samir" means storyteller and tale-teller. This can be the object of "tahjuroon", and if it is established as the state (hal) of the pronoun of the preposition, there is no defect. Many examples of establishing the state from the preposition have been presented in this book.

"Tahjuroon" is used in its well-known meaning (you leave/abandon). There is no need to remove it from its well-known meaning.

If you are content with this structure of speech, the matter said from "la taj'aroo al-yawma" until here, in simple words, is: "Now, cry or wail, nothing is obtained from it; now, neither is there any hearing for you in Our presence, nor can anyone else deliver you from Us. The time to obtain Our pleasure was when Our verses were being recited to you by the tongue of Our Messenger, but in that time, the state of your bad-brainedness was: with extreme arrogance, mocking it, you avoided it in such a manner as if he were some storyteller whose words, in your view, are not worthy of attention."

It should be kept in mind: the past stories the Messenger (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) recited to the comfortable ones—instead of obtaining lesson from them, they mocked you and instigated their common folk: "What has this man! Some tales of the past which he is reciting! 'In hadha illa asateeru al-awwaleen'".

The upcoming theme... Verses 68-92:
Forward, in some verses, there is warning and rebuke on the careless attitude of the disbelievers. Then comfort is given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Do not be hopeless of the obstinacy of these people. You are on the Straight Path, and these people are astray from the Straight Path. They will see their end with their own eyes. Even if We put these people into some calamity as a warning, they are not to improve; rather, as soon as they are free of it, they will fall again into their intoxications." After that, attention is directed toward the signs of the horizons and the souls, so that people may leave their careless attitude and turn to thinking, but in reply to this too, they said: "All these are tales of the past." After that, in some verses, attention is directed to the contradiction in people's thinking, asking: "On one hand, you acknowledge and announce correct things, but then where does your intellect go that you believe in completely opposite things, wandering in the valley of misguidance!"... In this light, recite the verses.

Interrogative style with the purpose of rebuke and blame:
This and the subsequent matters in the interrogative style are all by way of expressing sorrow and with the purpose of rebuke and blame. By "qawl" (word) here is meant the Quran, whose turning away has been mentioned in the above verse. He said: "After all, why are these people turning away from this Quran in this manner! Have they not reflected over this Word, or is it their assumption that this is contrary to the way of their ancestors?" Since the speech is in the tone of rebuke and sorrow, the matter is left brief. In another place, there is the specification: their hearts are sealed; for this reason, the words of the Quran do not enter their hearts. Considering the Quran contrary to the traditions of ancestors and turning away from it is also sheer ignorance. The way of ancestors is not in itself the proof of its correctness; rather, its standing on the scale of intellect and nature is also necessary; besides, why do these people not see beyond their nose! Their original great ancestor is Hazrat Ibrahim and Hazrat Ismail—they were the carriers and inviting ones of this very religion. Why do these people not follow these great ancestors of theirs, instead of following the track of those ancestors who were completely ignorant of the religion!

Warning to the Quraysh of a severe danger:
In the light of the Divine Sunnah explained in detail in the Quran, this is a warning to the Quraysh of a severe danger. That Divine Sunnah is: Allah sends a messenger to every nation for the completion of proof, who is from among themselves. If the nation believes in him, Allah exalts that nation and grants it power in the land; and if it denies the messenger, after the completion of proof, it is inevitably destroyed. For this reason, the stage of the mission of a messenger inside a nation is a highly delicate stage. In this stage alone is the decision of its life or death. Wretched is that nation which does not understand the delicacy of this stage and displays a careless attitude in the case of its messenger. On this naci-aqibat-andishee of the Quraysh, this is a warning: "Have they not recognized their messenger, so they are denying him, or is everything happening knowingly, with vision? If it is happening with vision, the end of this game is known!"

The reason for declaring the messenger crazy is in reality aversion to truth:
That is, if they say to make their common folk foolish: "The messenger is afflicted with crazy and madness," this is proof of their own madness. The messenger has no crazy or madness. What he is presenting is completely truth, and his every single word will be fulfilled. However, they themselves are averse to the truth. For this reason, they are declaring the messenger crazy. When the diagnosis of the doctor seems bitter to the patient, and he is not ready to swallow the medicine, instead of accepting himself as sick, he declares the doctor's advice as delirium. This same was the state of these people. They were not ready to leave their falsehood and swallow the bitter drafts of truth; for this reason, they declared the messenger crazy, to put some veil over their own loss of intellect.

There is a great distance between the desires of the soul and the truth:
Now, the different temperaments of the desires of the soul and the truth are clarified: there is a great distance between the two. The demands of the desires of the soul are something else; the requirements of the truth are completely separate, something else. If the truth were to follow their desires, as they want—that the words of the Quran and the Prophet be according to their desires, only then they will believe—its inevitable result would be the destruction of this entire universe. This is because they are the standard-bearers of evil instead of good, wrong instead of justice, bad instead of virtue, treachery instead of trust, and more than this, Shirk instead of Monotheism and denial of Resurrection instead of Resurrection. If all these values were turned upside down according to their desires, the entire moral system of this world would be disrupted; and if the existence of Shirk were accepted in it, the system of this universe could not remain even for a single day with Shirk. This reality is clarified forward in this Surah:
(Allah has not taken any son, nor has there ever been with Him any deity. [If there had been], then each deity would have taken what it created, and some of them would have sought to overcome others.) [Al-Mu'minun: 91]

The meaning is: these people want to make the truth follow their desires, but the salvation and success of this world is in following the truth. The Creator of the universe cannot leave His world to their mercy. Now, let these people decide themselves: do they want life by following the truth, or death and destruction by remaining associated with falsehood! If life is desired, these bitter drafts must be swallowed, whether they seem bitter or sweet. Apart from this, there is no other path of life.

The end of those who turn away from the reminder:
"Bal ataynahum bidhikrihim fahum 'an dhikrihim mu'ridoon"—this is, in other words, the same warning which passed in "Am lam ya'rifoo rasoolahum" above. The meaning is: if they consider this Quran as the tale-telling of some storyteller, they are assuming very wrongly. This is that reminder of their portion which We have sent to them for the completion of proof. If they do not benefit from this reminder, let them remember: their end too will be the same as was of the nations before them who did not appreciate God's reminder.
