Above, we have referenced that Divine Sunnah: Allah sent His messengers to every nation, and through these messengers, He reminded those nations. Whichever nation appreciated this reminder, obtained success; whichever did not appreciate it, was destroyed. Pointing to this Divine Sunnah here, He warned: "These people are turning away from this reminder, and are unaware of its consequences!"

"Am tas'aluhum kharjan fakharaju rabbika khayrun wa huwa khayru ar-raziqeen" [72].

In this verse, there is comfort for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and blame for his opponents. The meaning is: "You are not demanding any reward for what you are presenting before them, that they are burdened under it—you received it free, and are distributing it free. If they did not appreciate this blessing, it is their own deprivation. If they assume: they can harm you by not accepting your call, they are assuming completely wrongly. The reward granted by your Lord is better for you, and He is the best provider of favor." The messenger of God is no trader or merchant; he is a caller to truth. His call carries its own provision, and this same is the status of those who work on the path of the prophets.

Verses 73-74:
The actual cause of deviation from the Straight Path:
That is: "As far as your call is concerned, it is the call to walk on the correct highway of life. By walking on this highway alone, man can reach his destination, but to adopt it, it is necessary that a person believe in the Hereafter. If he does not believe in the Hereafter, what has happened to him to keep watch on the freedoms and unbridlednesses of his soul? Only he will be able to sacrifice his present pleasures who is waiting for a Day of Reward. One who is not waiting for any such day will follow only his desires, no matter how far their pursuit may take him from the Straight Path." In this verse, this reality is clarified to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): the reason why those who are deviating from your shown path are doing so is not because there is some bend in it—this is a completely straight path—but whatever defect there is, is in their hearts. These people do not believe in the Hereafter, and those who do not believe in the Hereafter will inevitably walk crookedly, deviating from this straight path. Keep in mind this reality of the wisdom of religion: unless you believe in the Hereafter alongside this world, neither will any part of this universe seem straight to you, nor will the correct highway of life be determined. We are explaining this reality in places.

Comfort to the Prophet in the light of history:
In this verse, in accordance with the well-known style of Arabic, a part of the condition is omitted based on contextual clarity. If you open this omission, the entire matter will be: "If We, as a warning, put them into some calamity so that some fear of God and humility are born in them, then We showed mercy upon them and removed their calamity, they would again return to remain in their rebellion, wandering." The meaning is: to this type of people, even the signs of punishment which they demand do not bring any benefit. If some bend is born in their necks because of them, it is born only temporarily. The days of calamity pass, and their leaders begin to teach this lesson: such soft and hard situations do happen to nations—what connection does this have with our faith and morals!... This is to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that he should not give any importance to their demand for a sign of punishment.

To the people of this character, no sign brings benefit.

Warning punishment:
"Wa laqad akhadhnahum"—meaning, We grasped their counterparts before them in Our warning punishments, but neither did any humility and self-abasement arise in their hearts, nor did they pray and cry before their Lord. This, in the light of past history, is the argument for the matter mentioned in the above verse: that history is witness that people of this character do not obtain any lesson from warnings. The meaning is: if they too are warned, the Prophet must not expect that they will obtain any lesson from it. "Hum" (them) here refers not to the Quraysh but to their counterparts who passed in the previous nations. In Arabic, in cases of extreme similarity, pronouns come in this manner. In this is the eloquence: grasping them was as grasping these, because both committed the same crime.

There is a difference of within and without between "istikanah" and "tadarru'". "Istikanah" is the expression of the heart's humility, and "tadarru'" refers to that crying and praying which manifests as "istikanah". In my view, before the imperfect verb, a past-tense helping verb is assumed as omitted here.

"Hatta idha fatahna 'alayhim baban dha 'adhabin shadeedin idha hum feehi mublisoon" (Until when We have opened upon them a gate of severe punishment, immediately they are therein in despair).

Meaning of "'Adhab Shadeed":
That is: "With warning jolts, the minds of these arrogant ones are not to improve. Until that time comes when, in the consequence of this denial of theirs, We open upon them the gates of Our severe punishment." By "'adhab shadeed" (severe punishment) here is meant that decisive punishment which, under the Divine Sunnah, came upon every nation that denied its messenger and persisted in its obstinacy. The explanation of this Divine Sunnah has passed in places in this book. After the manifestation of this punishment, the nation is given no further respite. All its supports and hopes are finished at once. To this reality, the final words of the verse point.

The actual purpose of ear, eye, and heart:
In the previous speech, the address was to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and the warning to the Quraysh was with the third-person pronoun. Now, addressing them directly, He said: "This which God has granted you ear, eye, and heart—for what has He granted them! For this alone: that through them, you hear those words of advice and wisdom which are being recited to you; you see those signs of Allah which are spread in the horizons and the souls, to which your attention is being directed; and you ponder over those results which emerge from them, which are being presented to you clearly. The actual purpose of ear, eye, and heart is this, but you are strange wretched people that to believe in the truth, instead of argument and reasoning, you are demanding the blow of warning!" The meaning is: if instead of obtaining guidance from intellect and reasoning, a person is convinced only by the logic of the blow, what is the difference between him and an ass!

"Qaleelan ma tashkuroon" is a phrase of expressing sorrow and regret. The meaning is: "You receive such high blessings of Allah, but appreciate them very little!" The actual reality of gratitude, as explained in its place, is appreciation of the blessing, and the appreciation of the blessing is that a person benefits from it correctly and is grateful to his Giver.

A comprehensive argument of Monotheism and Resurrection:
This has directed attention to Resurrection and gathering: "Do not be in this delusion that you are just born here and will turn to dust here. When a farmer sows in his field, he does not sow merely for the sake of sowing; rather, one day he gathers it too. Similarly, He who has spread you in this earth will also gather you one day—so remember: you will be gathered only in His field. After all, how can any other associate in His sown crop!" Ponder: how beautifully, inside these few words, the possibility of Resurrection is pointed to, the necessity of the Return explained, and the argument of Monotheism clarified.

Some pointers of the alternation of night and day:
By "ikhtilaf al-layli wan-nahari" (alternation of night and day) is meant the coming and going of day and night one after another, with complete punctuality of time. To this sign, the Quran has directed attention in places.

Their coming and going with complete punctuality of time is proof: they are subjected in the hand of the Creator of this universe. They are authorized neither to come nor to go of their own accord.

No one being able to interfere in their system is testimony: the actual administrator is Allah alone, without partners.

Their compatibility, despite difference of temperament and shape, for the nourishment of this universe is testimony: in this universe, the will of a single Wise and Powerful is active, who creates connection between its opposites and uses them for the collective benefit of the universe.

Rising in the morning after the sleep of rest in the night is a reminder of Resurrection for every human being: in this manner, after the sleep of barzakh, when Allah wills, everyone will rise.

Besides these, other pointers too have been extracted from the alternation of night and day by the Quran, and we have tried to explain them in this book. Pointing to them, He said here: "He who gives life and death, and in whose hand of control is the alternation of night and day—He is your True Deity, with Him you have to deal, and to Him you are being called—so where are you being turned! Is such a clear reality not entering your understanding!"

Verses 81-83:
The same old answer:
That is: "All these things are clear and proved in their place, but they are not entering their hearts. They said, after hearing all this, exactly what their early counterparts said. They said: 'When we die and turn to dust and bones, will we be raised alive again? This is an impossible thing! This warning is being warned to us, and before this, this same warning was warned to our predecessors too, but Resurrection neither came nor came nor will come. All these are tales of the past which are being repeated. We are not to fall for these warnings!'"

Verses 84-85:
The contradiction in the thinking of the deniers:
Now, the contradiction in the thinking of these deniers is being highlighted: on one hand, they believe in the Creator, Owner, and Absolute Controller of heaven and earth to be God alone; on the other hand, they believe in things which are completely opposite to it, or deny things which hold the status of the inevitable result of this belief of theirs. Regarding the Arab polytheists, we have explained in its place: they believed in the Creator, Owner, and Administrator of heaven and earth to be God alone, but along with this, they were involved in those types of Shirk whose explanation has passed in places. As for the Resurrection, first of all they considered it a highly far-fetched thing, and if they believed in it as a hypothesis, their idea was: their intercessors would save them from God's grasp. If you ponder, you will realize: this is the same contradiction in thinking in which generally the Muslims of today are involved. They acknowledge God too, and also deny the inevitable results of this acknowledgment. They claim faith too, and at the same time, believe in and do things which are completely contrary to faith.

This is a direction to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): ask these claimants of knowledge: to whom belongs this earth and whoever is in it, if you know. Within "in kuntum ta'lamoon" (if you know), there is a kind of sarcasm on their contradiction in thinking, because the knowledge which is a collection of contradictory thoughts is not knowledge but ignorance, and ignorance of a severe kind. He said: "The answer to your question they will inevitably give is: all these things belong to Allah alone. Then say to them: 'Afala tadhakkaroon'—in the light of this belief of yours, why do you not review your thoughts and beliefs? After this, how do you deny Monotheism? From where did you invent these partners and intercessors? Why does Resurrection seem a strange thing to you, and why do you turn away from the call of the messenger in this manner!"

Verses 86-87:
He said: "Ask them: who is the Lord of the seven heavens and the Lord of the Great Throne?" The above question was related to the earth and the residents of earth; this question is related to the seven heavens and the Great Throne. The Great Throne represents the all-encompassing absolute power of Allah. The Arabs, just as they believed in the Creator and Owner of the earth to be Allah alone, believed in the Owner of the seven heavens and the Great Throne also to be Allah alone. They, unlike other polytheistic nations of the world, were not believers in separate deities of the heavens and the earth. He said: "They will give the answer to this question of yours also that all these things belong to Allah alone." Since the actual meaning of "man rabbu as-samawati as-sab'i" is: whose sovereignty is in them, for this reason, in its answer, "lillahi" (to Allah) is completely appropriate. He said: "If they give the answer to this question of yours also in the affirmative, then ask them: 'Do you not fear the Lord of the seven heavens and the Great Throne, that you commit transgression in His sovereignty, violate His commands and laws, and associate others in His sovereignty without any right! If His anger and wrath descend upon you, after all, who is there who can hold His hand.'"

Verses 88-89:
Meaning of "Malakoot":
"Malakoot" here is in the meaning of the rein of choice and power. That is: "Ask them: who is He in whose hand is the rein of everything, and His majesty is: He can protect whomever He wishes, but no other has this majesty that he can deliver anyone from His grasp?" He said: "The answer to this also they will inevitably give is: all these powers are in the hand of Allah." The polytheists of Arabia, regarding the deities they worshipped, did not have this conception that they possessed such choice and power that they could save anyone from God's grasp by their power; rather, they worshipped the angels, assuming them the beloved daughters of God, only in this hope: if they remained pleased, through them, God would remain pleased with them. Along with this question also are the words "in kuntum ta'lamoon". The meaning is: "In the mythology you have prepared, you did not give this rank to anyone, but if there is anyone and you know and believe in him, just name him." Then He said: "If they do not accept the rein of power to be in the hand of anyone other than God, ask them: 'Who has blown magic on you, that your intellect is completely lost, and you leave God to call upon others.'"

Verses 90-92:
Correction on the mentioned questions and answers:
Now, there is a correction on all this questioning and answering: "All these things they believe in are completely contrary to their own confessions; for this reason, what We have presented in this Quran is truth, and they are completely liars, because they themselves are denying themselves through their own mouth. God has taken no son, nor any daughter, and there is no partner with Him. If deities were associated in the creation of this universe, every deity would have taken what it created and departed, and would have made an attack upon one another, and this entire system would have been ruined long ago." Such absurdities which they describe are completely contrary to the high attributes of God. Allah is the Knower of all unseen and present; so what is His need to make anyone His partner. He is far above those things which they associate with Him.

In the mythology of the Greeks and Hindus, their deities' bloody wars are mentioned—keep them in view here, and also keep in view the ancient Greeks' belief that the man was created by the sun, and the woman by the earth. Ponder: if the sun were to depart with the men created by it, what would be the state of the women of the earth! The Quran has directed attention to this reality: this workshop of the universe is established only because the will of a single Creator and Owner is active in it. If other deities were also associated in it, its survival would have been impossible.

The upcoming theme... Verses 93-118:
Forward are the concluding verses of the Surah, in which glad tidings are given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): now the time of victory and help is near. The believers will be successful and victorious, and your opponents will face humiliation in both this world and the Hereafter; so, tolerate their mischief for a while. The hour of decision is right on the head. The term they consider very long, and in whose deception they are becoming daring in their mischief, after the end appears, they will feel that it passed in the blink of an eye, and at that time, they will beat their heads on their wretchedness. In this light, recite the verses.

Verses 93-94:
Glad tidings regarding the promise of punishment:
Regarding the punishment that comes upon a nation in the case of denying the messenger, when the arrogant of Quraysh were warned of it, they demanded from the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "If you are truthful, bring that punishment." The answer to this demand of theirs has been given in places in the Quran. For example, in Surah Yunus, it is said: (And whether We show you some of what We promise them or at ourselves take you, to Us is their return) [Yunus: 46]. Similarly, in Surah Ar-Ra'd, He said: (And whether We show you some of what We promise them or at ourselves take you, upon you is only the duty of notification, and upon Us is the account) [Ar-Ra'd: 40]. The exact same theme is in verse 77 of Surah Ghafir. From these verses, it is known: as far as the promise of punishment for the deniers is concerned, it is certain. As for the time of its manifestation—whether it will manifest during the Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) life, or after your death—this question's answer was left ambiguous in those verses, but the verse under discussion points to the fact: the time of the manifestation of dominance and victory for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions is now very near. This will occur during your life, and its inevitable result is also the destruction of your enemies. This pointer emerges from this: in it, this prayer is taught to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Pray: 'O Lord! If You are to show me that punishment during my life which they are being warned of, then keep me under Your mantle of mercy; do not include me among the wrongdoers.'" Within this prayer, the glad tiding for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the final warning for his deniers are completely clear, and here the reality also is: your enemies were pampal-ed during your life, and when you departed from this world, you departed after making the announcement: (Truth has come, and falsehood has departed) [Al-Isra: 81].

"Wa-inna 'ala an nuriyaka ma na'iduhum laqadiroon" (And indeed, We are powerful to show you what We promise them) [95].

Allah's power against all unfavorable situations:
This has confirmed the glad tiding implicit in the above verse. This was because in this period, the situations were still very unfavorable. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions, tired of the oppressions of the adversaries, were forced to migrate from Makkah. It was not easy to believe that from inside this darkness, light was soon to be born. To remove this hesitation, Allah said: "We are fully powerful over destroying your enemies during your life. Situations do not obstruct Our intention."

Teaching this prayer to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is not only representing the intensity of this punishment, as common people have assumed; rather, in it is also the pointer to migration: "O Lord! If this punishment is to come during my life, then before its arrival, open the path of salvation for me and my believing companions." As if in the style of prayer, you were also given this glad tiding: the means to protect you from this punishment has already been made by Allah.

Direction to pardon and forgive:
This is the direction of patience, pardon, and forgiveness to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Continue to answer their bad behavior with your good behavior. The harsa-sara'ees and bad-mannerednesses they are doing, We are well-aware of them." The meaning is: "We will make them taste the consequence of their every single mischief; do not be disheartened by their attitude. Leave their matter to Us. Now, the time of decision is near."

Verses 97-98:
Prayer for acquiring patience, pardon, and forgiveness:
This is the prayer taught to acquire patience, pardon, and forgiveness. By "Satan" is meant the devils of jinn who make whisperings in the hearts, and also the devils of humans whose attitude is under discussion here. He said: "Continue to pray: 'O Lord! Protect me from the whisperings of the devils of jinn, and also protect me from these devils of humans, lest they come to me for debate and creating mischief.'"

Verses 99-100:
The belated regret of the disbelievers:
That is: "These people are not to turn back from these mischiefs of theirs, until when death comes to one of them, and he deals with the states and matters of that world, only then his eyes will open. At that time, he will request with great humbleness: 'O Lord! Let me return to the world once, so that I may do some good in what I have left behind.' 'Kalla innaha kalimatun huwa qa'iluha wa min wara'ihim barzakhun...'—He said: 'This is not to happen under any circumstances. This is merely a word he will say with great regret, but the time for this regret to be fulfilled has passed; the respite of action is only until the life of the world. After this life is finished, before them will be a veil which will rise only on that day when they are raised for account.'" This segment can also mean: he will say this word, but if he were sent back to the world, he would do exactly what he had been doing; parallels of this meaning are present in the Quran, but in our view, the meaning is what we have chosen. Its highly clear parallels are present in the last group of surahs.

Verse 101:
The self-concern of the Day of Resurrection:
This is the picture of the self-concern of the Day of Resurrection: "On that day, neither anyone's lineage will be of any use, nor will they be able to ask one another for help." "Tasa'ul" means to ask one another for help. At the time of calamity, lineage-family bias and national-tribal mutual support is a great support in this world, and to this, the Arabs specifically accorded great importance. When a person cried out the name of his family or tribe, every individual of his tribe stood up to die for his support. He said: "After the trumpet is blown, all lineages will be finished, and neither will anyone be able to ask one another for help, nor will anyone be able to help anyone."

Verses 102-104:
On that day, no one's lineage will be of any use, but only his action will be of use. Those whose scales are heavy will be the successful, and those whose scales are light are the ones who put themselves into loss—they will abide in Hell forever. In that Hell, their state will be: the fire will ঝلس their faces, and they will be therein with distorted lips.

Verses 105-107:
The cry of the disbelievers and its answer:
That is, when they cry out, they will receive this answer from Allah: "Now why do you weep? To warn you of this day, when Our verses were recited to you, you denied them!" They will argue: "O Lord! Our wretchedness and misfortune overcame us, so we denied this day; indeed, we became wrongdoers to our own souls, but O our Lord, give us one more opportunity. If we do this again, indeed, we are the ones worthy of punishment."

"Qala ikhsa'oo feeha wa la tukallimoon" [108].

The word "ikhsa'" is used to dismiss a dog. The meaning is: they will receive this answer from Allah to this entreaty of theirs: "Go, remain humiliated in it, and do not speak to Me now."

Verses 109-111:
Dildari to the poor Muslims:
This is the description of that honor due to which the Jealous God will stop these wretched ones from speaking. The meaning is: "Now do not open your mouth before Me; you are those wretched and unfortunate ones: when a group of My servants prayed before Me saying: 'Our Lord! We have believed, so forgive us and have mercy upon us, and You are the best of those who show mercy,' you made them an object of mockery, and in this occupation of mockery, you remained so absorbed that you did not even remember that you have to deal with Me too one day, and you kept making fun of them with great audacity—so today, I have rewarded them for their patience and steadfastness, and now they have attained their goal, and you suffer this punishment and burn in it!"

In "fareequn min 'ibadee" (a group of My servants), the word "fareeq" points to the fact: by this is meant specifically the poor Muslims who, because of their poverty and helplessness, were the target of the oppression, mockery, and humbug of the arrogant of Quraysh. He said: "They prayed to Us for forgiveness and mercy, and you, because of your arrogance, made their prayer and prayer an occupation of recreation." "Sukhriyy" refers to that person or thing who is made an object of laughter and mockery. Making someone an object of mockery is in itself a very great arrogance, and this arrogance becomes multiple if some wretched one mocks his prayer and prayer, because in this form, he not only mocks him but also mocks God Himself.

"Hatta ansawkum dhikree"—meaning, in this occupation of recreation, you remained so absorbed that you never got the opportunity to think: there is also your God, who is observing these actions of yours which you are committing with His remembering, praising, patient, and grateful servants, and one day He will surely take revenge for every single thing of these.

"Innee jazaytuhum al-yawma bima sabaroo annahum humu al-fa'izoon"—meaning, now open your eyes and see, that thing is before you which you forgot. Despite all your oppression, wrong, mockery, and humbug, my servants who remained firm on My worship—I have rewarded them today for their steadfastness. "Annahum humu al-fa'izoon"—this is the detail of this reward: that they alone became the successful ones, from which this point emerged spontaneously: those who mocked them became the unsuccessful ones. Here, refresh in the mind the first verse of this Surah "Qad aflaha al-mu'minoon alladhina hum fee salatihim khashi'oon". On the very word with which the Surah began, the Surah is concluding. This style of returning to the beginning is used much in the Quran, and is of the highly clear arguments of the order of the Quran.

Ponder: what effect would these verses have had on those poor Muslims who, in that period, were the target of the oppression and humbug of the arrogant! How much food and strength their patience and determination would have obtained from this dildari of the Jealous Lord!

Verses 112-114:
The delusion of the length of life:
Now, it is clarified: this which they considered the manifestation of Resurrection a highly far-fetched thing, under the delusion: "even if it comes, there is so much delay in its coming that there is no need to disturb one's luxury in its worry from now"—this delusion will very soon be removed. After the trumpet is blown, when they rise, they will feel: the life they considered very long was finished in the blink of an eye.

The subject of "qala" (He said) here is not Allah or some angel, as some have assumed; rather, this is in the meaning of "qala qa'ilun" (a speaker said). That is, on being raised at the Resurrection, some speaker from among themselves will ask: "Tell, how long did we stay in the world?" Others will answer: "Just a day or even less than a day. 'Fas'ali al-'addeen' (so ask those who keep count)—this will be the expression of aversion from the side of those answering: 'Who knows how many days we stayed in the world! If anyone kept count of it, ask him!'" That is, the life on whose length and luxury they are pleased today, one day is coming when any question regarding it will also seem highly heavy to the nature. "Qala in labithtum illa qaleelan law annakum kuntum ta'lamoon"—this also will be the statement of someone among themselves, in which there is some meaning of blame and regret: "Indeed, you stayed in the world very little, but the time to know and understand this was when you were in the world; now, its time has passed."

This same theme has passed in verses 102-104 of Surah Ta-Ha too. There is the specification: these talks will be among themselves. (They will whisper among themselves, "You remained not but ten [days]") [Ta-Ha: 103]. In this connection, the statement of their most wise and sensible is also quoted: (when the best of them in manner will say, "You remained not but a day") [Ta-Ha: 104]. In my view, in the verses under discussion too, the final statement of their "amthaluhum tareeqatan" is quoted.

In Surah Al-Kahf, verse 19, the dialogue mentioned of the companions of the Cave after rising from the long sleep—which, in reality, is an example of the life after the barzakh life—from that too it is known that this questioning and answering will be among themselves. This is a reality in which there is no exaggeration: the entire length and breadth of the life of heedlessness is felt only as long as it is obtained; when it passes, it seems merely a dream!

"It was a transaction with you in the dream of imagination,
When the eyes opened, there was neither loss nor gain."

"Afahasibtum annama khalaqnakum 'abathan wa-annakum ilayna la turja'oon" (Then did you think that We created you uselessly and that to Us you would not be returned?) [115].

Reward and punishment are the inevitable demand of the attributes of Allah:
That is: "If you are sitting indifferent to reward and punishment, have you assumed: We have created you without any purpose, and you will continue to wander like this unbridled, and will die one day, and your return to Us is not to be?" The meaning is: "If you assumed so, you assumed very wrongly regarding Us."

Verse 116:
This is the reminder of His attributes, whose purpose is the refutation of that delusion in which the deniers of reward and punishment were involved. He said: "The essence of God is highly high and exalted. From the majesty of this high and exalted essence, this is highly far-fetched: He should perform some useless work and create an entire world merely as a plaything. He is Al-Malik al-Haqq—meaning, the True Sovereign of this universe—so how is it possible that He should not establish justice among His subjects, making the wrongdoer and the oppressed equal. 'La ilaha illa huwa'—meaning, there is no other deity besides Him—so let no one remain in this delusion: he can deliver himself from His grasp through the effort and intercession of someone else. 'Rabbi al-'arshi al-kareem'—the Throne represents the sovereignty of God. And the word 'kareem' has entered in the Quran in the meaning of honored and generous. The meaning is: God is the Lord of an honored and generous Throne, so how can His government be a place of darkness!"

Verse 117:
This has further clarified the above theme of Monotheism: "Whoever associates someone else in His worship alongside Allah, while there is no argument in favor of any partner, let him remember: in the presence of God, he has to give his account one day, and let him also remember: those who associate someone with God are disbelievers, and disbelievers will not obtain success." On "Qad aflaha al-mu'minoon", this Surah began, and on "la yuflihu al-kafiroon", it concluded.

"Wa-qul rabbi ighfir warham wa-anta khayru ar-rahimeen" (And, [O Muhammad], say, "My Lord, forgive and have mercy, and You are the best of those who show mercy") [118].

Glad tidings of victory in the style of prayer:
At the end, the prayer of seeking forgiveness and mercy is taught to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and through him to his companions, and if you ponder, you will realize: this prayer is the same which was referenced in verse 109, and which the arrogant mocked. From the teaching of this prayer, this pointer emerges: stand firm on your position, and continue to make this prayer. This is the key of the gate of forgiveness and mercy for you. This is, in the style of prayer, glad tidings of the manifestation of victory for the believers.

On these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. O Allah, forgive and show mercy, and You are the best of those who show mercy.
